summary.hook: This poster presents a policy translation effort to expand breastfeeding and lactation support in Arizona by addressing gaps in Medicaid coverage for outpatient lactation services.
summary.summary_30s: This poster presents a policy translation effort to expand breastfeeding and lactation support in Arizona by addressing gaps in Medicaid coverage for outpatient lactation services. Guided by a systems change framework, the team translated evidence on structural gaps into two bills: HB 2051 (Medicaid coverage of breastfeeding/lactation services across settings) and HB 2072 (state certification program for lactation care providers). The poster summarizes a policy gap review (Medicaid State Plan Amendments, 2012–2024) showing 14 states with explicit lactation coverage provisions and notes Arizona lacks formal Medicaid reimbursement mechanisms for outpatient lactation services, then tracks the legislative progress of both bills through early March 2026.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Translate research-identified structural gaps into legislative action to advance breastfeeding support in Arizona via two bills (HB 2051, HB 2072).

Approach: Population: Medicaid policy documents (State Plan Amendments) and Arizona legislative process (bills HB 2051 & HB 2072). Primary outcome: Legislative progress of HB 2051 and HB 2072 (stage completion and votes).
Finding: A comprehensive SPA review (Jan 2012–Dec 2024) identified 14 states with explicit Medicaid provisions for lactation coverage and reimbursement.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Not stated on poster as a formal conclusion section; the poster’s narrative implies that closing Medicaid coverage/reimbursement gaps via legislation will expand lactation support and improve maternal/infant outcomes, particularly for underserved communities. Implications (poster-grounded): Establishing Medicaid coverage (HB 2051) and provider certification infrastructure (HB 2072) are positioned as levers to expand access to lactation services. The gap in Medicaid outpatient lactation coverage is framed as an equity issue disproportionately affecting low-income/underserved communities. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Policy impact pathway: certification → provider recognition → Medicaid enrollment → reimbursement authority → expanded support → healthier families. Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Meek & Noble (2022) Pediatrics policy statement on breastfeeding/human milk. HRSA (2025) women’s preventive services guidelines. Webber & Benedict (2015) on billing for lactation services. CMS (n.d.) Medicaid State Plan Amendments resource.
key_numbers: Not stated on poster (policy review yields “14 states” with explicit provisions).
SPA review: Jan 2012–Dec 2024; legislative lifecycle: 2025–2026 with dated steps in Jan–Mar 2026.
Medicaid policy documents (State Plan Amendments) and Arizona legislative process (bills HB 2051 & HB 2072).
SPA review (2012–2024) found 14 states with explicit Medicaid lactation coverage/reimbursement provisions.
HB 2051 expands AHCCCS coverage of breastfeeding/lactation services across multiple settings.
HB 2072 creates a voluntary AZDHS certification program with 2-year certifications for lactation care providers.
takeaways: A comprehensive SPA review (Jan 2012–Dec 2024) identified 14 states with explicit Medicaid provisions for lactation coverage and reimbursement.
The poster states Arizona lacks formal Medicaid reimbursement mechanisms for outpatient lactation services.
Policy impact pathway (diagram): certification → provider recognition → Medicaid enrollment → reimbursement authority → expanded support → healthier families.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What was the exact coding framework for determining whether a state SPA included “explicit lactation coverage and reimbursement”?

what_to_ask.question: You found 14 states with explicit provisions—what common elements did those states share that you tried to replicate?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How did you ensure completeness of the SPA review—did you rely solely on CMS listings or cross-check with state Medicaid manuals?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: If both bills pass, what changes would a Medicaid beneficiary actually notice when seeking lactation help?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
